title: ACAS Guide: Managing Absence and Improving Performance
chapters: Non-statutory guidance · Published by ACAS · Covers absence management, sickness absence procedures, capability dismissals, performance improvement plans, and the interplay with disability discrimination obligations
desc: The ACAS guide on managing absence and improving performance provides practical guidance on handling short-term and long-term sickness absence, capability-based performance management, and the fair process for dismissing employees on grounds of capability (ERA 1996 s.98(2)(a)). Capability dismissals are distinct from misconduct dismissals — the employer must follow a supportive process focused on improvement rather than punishment. The guide covers return-to-work interviews, absence trigger points, occupational health referrals, performance improvement plans (PIPs), the duty to make reasonable adjustments for disabled employees under EqA 2010 s.20, and the interaction between long-term absence management and the unfair dismissal regime. Employers must be particularly careful when managing absence related to pregnancy, maternity, or disability, as dismissal for these reasons may be automatically unfair and/or discriminatory.
legalStatus: Non-statutory guidance. Tribunals regularly refer to ACAS guidance when assessing the fairness of capability dismissals. The ACAS Code of Practice on Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures applies to capability dismissals — the Code states that employers may address performance issues under a separate capability procedure but the basic principles of fairness must be followed.

sections.title: Absence policies, return-to-work interviews, and trigger points
sections.summary: Employers should have a clear absence management policy communicated to all employees. The policy should set out notification requirements (who to contact, when, and how), evidence requirements (self-certification for the first 7 days, fit note from GP from day 8), return-to-work interviews (recommended after every period of absence), trigger points for formal action (e.g. Bradford Factor, number of absences in a rolling period), and the process for managing persistent short-term absence and long-term absence. Return-to-work interviews are one of the most effective tools for managing absence — they demonstrate to the employee that their absence has been noticed, provide an opportunity to identify any underlying issues, and enable the employer to offer support. Trigger points should be applied consistently but employers must ensure they do not inadvertently discriminate against disabled employees or employees with pregnancy-related absence.
sections.text:
Employers should have a clear written absence management policy that covers:

• notification requirements: who the employee should contact, when (normally before their start time on the first day of absence), and how (phone call, not text/email)
• evidence requirements: self-certification for days 1–7; fit note from GP from day 8 onwards
• return-to-work interviews: should be held after every period of absence
• trigger points for formal absence management: these may be based on the number of absences, total days absent, or a combination (e.g. Bradford Factor)
• referral to occupational health: when and how referrals will be made
• the process for persistent short-term absence (frequent, unconnected absences)
• the process for long-term absence (single continuous absence)
• the link to the disciplinary/capability procedure for persistent absence

When applying trigger points, employers must:
• exclude pregnancy-related absence from trigger calculations (sex discrimination under EqA 2010 s.18)
• consider whether disability-related absence should be discounted or adjusted as a reasonable adjustment (EqA 2010 s.20)
• apply triggers consistently across the workforce
• ensure that absence management action is proportionate and supportive

leadingCases.name: Lynock v Cereal Packaging Ltd [1988] ICR 670
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that in cases of persistent short-term absence, the employer must look at the overall attendance record and the pattern of absences. The employer is entitled to take a firm view on persistent absences without conducting the medical investigation that would be required for long-term sickness absence. However, the employer must still warn the employee that continued absence at this level may result in dismissal.
leadingCases.significance: Established the distinct approach for persistent short-term absence versus long-term illness

leadingCases.name: East Lindsey District Council v Daubney [1977] ICR 566
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that before dismissing an employee for long-term sickness absence, the employer should take reasonable steps to discover the true medical position. This normally involves obtaining an up-to-date medical report. The employer should also consult with the employee about the medical position and the prospects of returning to work.
leadingCases.significance: Foundation authority on the duty to obtain medical evidence before dismissing for long-term ill health

refs: ERA 1996 s.98(2)(a) (capability as potentially fair reason for dismissal)
ERA 1996 s.98(4) (test of fairness)
EqA 2010 s.18 (pregnancy and maternity discrimination — pregnancy-related absence)
EqA 2010 s.15 (discrimination arising from disability — disability-related absence)
EqA 2010 s.20 (duty to make reasonable adjustments — may include adjusting absence triggers)
Social Security Contributions and Benefits Act 1992 ss.151–163 (Statutory Sick Pay)
https://www.acas.org.uk/absence-from-work

sections.title: Long-term absence: medical evidence, consultation, and dismissal
sections.summary: Where an employee is on long-term sickness absence, the employer should maintain regular contact (without being intrusive), obtain up-to-date medical evidence (normally through an occupational health referral), consult with the employee about the medical position and any adjustments that could facilitate a return, consider whether reasonable adjustments are required under EqA 2010 s.20 if the employee is disabled, consider whether suitable alternative employment is available, and only proceed to dismissal as a last resort where there is no reasonable prospect of a return to work within a reasonable timescale. The employer must balance the needs of the business against the employee's interest in continued employment. There is no fixed period after which dismissal becomes fair — it depends on the nature of the illness, the employee's length of service, the size of the employer, and the impact of the absence on the business.
sections.text:
For long-term sickness absence, the employer should:

• maintain regular and supportive contact with the employee
• obtain up-to-date medical evidence through occupational health or the employee's GP (with consent)
• consult with the employee about the medical prognosis and any steps that could help a return
• consider reasonable adjustments if the employee meets the EqA 2010 definition of disability
• consider a phased return to work
• consider suitable alternative employment if the employee cannot return to their original role
• allow the employee to be accompanied at formal meetings
• only dismiss where there is no reasonable prospect of return within a reasonable period

The key questions for a tribunal assessing fairness of a long-term absence dismissal are:
• was there adequate medical investigation?
• was the employee consulted about the medical position?
• were reasonable adjustments considered (if disability applies)?
• was suitable alternative employment explored?
• did the employer wait a reasonable period?
• was the impact on the business considered?

leadingCases.name: Spencer v Paragon Wallpapers Ltd [1977] ICR 301
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT established the key factors for assessing the reasonableness of dismissing for long-term ill health: the nature of the illness, the likely length of continuing absence, the need of the employer to have someone doing the work, and the circumstances of the case as a whole. There is a need to balance the employer's need for the work to be done against the employee's interest in continued employment.
leadingCases.significance: Established the balancing exercise for long-term ill-health dismissals

leadingCases.name: BS v Dundee City Council [2014] CSIH 91
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Session confirmed that the three essential elements of a fair long-term absence dismissal are: (1) the employer must carry out a reasonable investigation into the employee's medical condition; (2) the employer must consult with the employee giving them the opportunity to state their position; and (3) the employer must consider whether there are alternative jobs which the employee could perform.
leadingCases.significance: Scottish authority confirming the three essential elements of fair ill-health dismissal — investigate, consult, consider alternatives

leadingCases.name: Metroline Travel Ltd v Stoute [2015] UKEAT/0302/14
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT emphasised the importance of the duty to make reasonable adjustments when managing the absence of a disabled employee. An employer who dismisses a disabled employee for long-term sickness absence without first considering and implementing reasonable adjustments may be liable for both unfair dismissal and disability discrimination.
leadingCases.significance: Confirmed the intersection of unfair dismissal and disability discrimination in long-term absence cases

refs: ERA 1996 s.98(2)(a) (capability — ill health)
EqA 2010 s.6 (definition of disability)
EqA 2010 s.15 (discrimination arising from disability)
EqA 2010 s.20–21 (duty to make reasonable adjustments and failure to comply)
Access to Medical Reports Act 1988 (employee consent for medical reports)
https://www.acas.org.uk/absence-from-work

sections.title: Performance improvement plans, capability procedure, and fair dismissal
sections.summary: Where an employee's performance is below the required standard, the employer should address this through a supportive capability process rather than the misconduct disciplinary procedure. The employer should: identify the performance shortfall clearly and specifically, investigate whether there are any underlying reasons (personal problems, inadequate training, disability, workload), set clear and measurable improvement targets with realistic timescales (a Performance Improvement Plan or PIP), provide any necessary training, support, or reasonable adjustments, hold regular review meetings to assess progress, and issue formal warnings only after the supportive process has been exhausted. A typical capability process follows: informal discussion first, then Stage 1 (first written warning with PIP), Stage 2 (final written warning if insufficient improvement), and Stage 3 (dismissal meeting if performance remains unacceptable). The ACAS Code of Practice on Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures applies — the employee must be informed in writing, given the opportunity to respond, allowed to be accompanied, and given the right to appeal. The employer must ensure the performance standards applied are reasonable and achievable, and that the employee has been given adequate time and support to improve.
sections.text:
Managing poor performance fairly involves:

• identify the performance gap: be specific about what the standard is and where the employee is falling short
• investigate underlying causes: inadequate training, unclear expectations, personal problems, disability, workload issues, management failures
• informal stage: discuss the concerns with the employee and agree improvement steps before moving to formal action
• formal Stage 1: if informal steps are insufficient, hold a formal capability meeting; issue a first written warning with a Performance Improvement Plan (PIP) setting clear, measurable targets and a realistic review period (typically 4–12 weeks depending on the role)
• formal Stage 2: if improvement is insufficient at review, hold a further meeting and issue a final written warning with an extended or revised PIP
• formal Stage 3: if performance remains unacceptable after a reasonable period with support, the employer may proceed to a dismissal meeting

The ACAS Code of Practice applies throughout. At each stage:
• the employee must be informed in writing of the concerns and possible consequences
• a meeting must be held to discuss the concerns
• the employee must be allowed to be accompanied
• the decision must be communicated in writing
• the employee must have the right to appeal

leadingCases.name: Bevan Harris Ltd v Gair [1981] IRLR 520
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that when assessing capability, the employer must apply a fair and reasonable standard — the standard of a reasonably competent employee in that role, not the standard of the best performer. The employer must also consider whether the performance shortfall is due to the employee's inability (capability) or unwillingness (misconduct).
leadingCases.significance: Established the reasonable standard test for capability assessment

leadingCases.name: Sulemanji v Toughened Glass Ltd [1979] ICR 799
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that before dismissing for poor performance, the employer must warn the employee that their performance is unsatisfactory, specify in what respects improvement is needed, give the employee a reasonable opportunity to improve, and ensure the employee understands that dismissal may follow if there is no improvement.
leadingCases.significance: Established the warning, specification, opportunity, and consequence requirements for fair capability dismissal

leadingCases.name: Taylor v Alidair Ltd [1978] ICR 445
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that the range of reasonable responses test applies to capability dismissals. The question is not whether the tribunal considers the employee incompetent, but whether the employer could reasonably have concluded that the employee was not performing to an acceptable standard.
leadingCases.significance: Confirmed that the range of reasonable responses test applies to capability as well as misconduct dismissals

refs: ERA 1996 s.98(2)(a) (capability as potentially fair reason for dismissal)
ERA 1996 s.98(3) (meaning of capability — assessed by reference to skill, aptitude, health or any other physical or mental quality)
ERA 1996 s.98(4) (test of fairness)
ACAS Code of Practice 1 (Disciplinary and Grievance — applies to capability procedures)
TULRCA 1992 s.207A (25% adjustment for unreasonable failure to follow the Code)
EqA 2010 s.20 (reasonable adjustments for disabled employees — may include modified performance targets or additional support)
https://www.acas.org.uk/capability-procedures

pendingAmendments: No direct ERA 2025 amendments, but removal of unfair dismissal qualifying period will increase capability dismissal claims
Monitoring for interaction with disability discrimination duty to make reasonable adjustments

parliamentaryOrigin.statutoryBasis: Non-statutory guidance. Absence management and capability procedures are assessed for fairness under ERA 1996 s.98(4). The ACAS Code of Practice 1 (Disciplinary and Grievance) applies where absence or performance issues lead to disciplinary proceedings or dismissal.
parliamentaryOrigin.note: ACAS absence and performance guidance covers: short-term absence management, long-term sickness absence, return-to-work interviews, trigger points, occupational health referrals, capability procedures, performance improvement plans, and the interaction with disability discrimination under EqA 2010.

precedingDocuments.title: ACAS Code of Practice 1
precedingDocuments.significance: The Code applies when absence or performance issues escalate to formal disciplinary or capability proceedings.

precedingDocuments.title: Equality Act 2010 ss.6, 15, 20-21
precedingDocuments.significance: Disability discrimination, discrimination arising from disability, and the duty to make reasonable adjustments — all critical interactions with absence management.

leadingCases.name: BS v Dundee City Council [2014] CSIH 91
leadingCases.heldText: For long-term sickness absence dismissals: the employer must consult with the employee, obtain up-to-date medical evidence, and consider alternatives to dismissal before concluding that the employee is no longer capable.
leadingCases.significance: Leading Scottish authority on fair long-term sickness absence dismissal procedures.

leadingCases.name: Lynock v Cereal Packaging Ltd [1988] ICR 670
leadingCases.heldText: For persistent short-term absence: the employer must look at the pattern, give warnings, consider underlying medical conditions, and assess whether the level of absence is one the business can sustain.
leadingCases.significance: Foundation authority for managing persistent short-term absence.

leadingCases.name: Aylott v Stockton-on-Tees Borough Council [2010] ICR 1278
leadingCases.heldText: The employer must not make stereotypical assumptions about an employee's mental health. Medical evidence must be obtained and the duty to make reasonable adjustments considered.
leadingCases.significance: No stereotypical assumptions about mental health — medical evidence required.
